152	# ── Gate 1 ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
153	
154	
155	def gate_1_node(state: HCGRCState) -> dict[str, Any]:
156	    """
157	    Gate 1 — Pre-data checkpoint.
158	
159	    Fires before any SCF data enters the system. The operator verifies that
160	    all Phase 0 prerequisites are complete (ADR-0015, #71):
161	
162	      1. LangGraph skeleton running
163	      2. Gate 1 fires with synthetic payload (this test)
164	      3. Data split idempotency verified (SHA-256 seed reproducible)
165	      4. PostgresSaver benchmark complete (throughput on target hardware)
166	      5. Governance dry-run complete (full escalation loop exercised)
167	      6. Diversity anchor models identified (T-01, ADR-0013)
168	
169	    The interrupt() surfaces the proposal and parks the graph until the
170	    operator responds. No SCF data is loaded before this gate approves.
171	    """
172	    checklist = [
173	        "LangGraph skeleton compiles and graph executes on synthetic payload",
174	        "Gate 1 interrupt fires and resolves correctly",
175	        "compute_data_split() produces identical splits on two independent runs (SHA-256 seed)",
176	        "PostgresSaver write/read benchmark complete — throughput logged to MLflow",
177	        "Governance dry-run complete — escalation loop exercised end-to-end",
178	        # ── Preregistration Ledger entries (logged before Phase 1 data ingestion) ──
179	        "LEDGER-0002: Diversity anchor model candidates logged (T-01 — 3 general + 2 anchors)",
180	        "LEDGER-0003: Fleiss kappa path selection logged (path 1: recruit raters | path 2: acknowledge unestimated)",
181	        # ── NOTE: DIVERGENCE-01 is a Gate 2 prerequisite, NOT Gate 1 ──────────────
182	        # DIVERGENCE-01 (operationalization of model-STRM divergence) requires the
183	        # STRM label distribution from EDA — which has not run yet at Gate 1.
184	        # Decision framework is defined in SAP §10. Specific operationalization is
185	        # selected post-EDA, logged to Preregistration Ledger before Gate 2.
186	        # Confirmed: SAP §10 DIVERGENCE-01 decision framework understood and acknowledged",
187	    ]
188	
189	    proposal = _build_gate_proposal(
190	        gate_id="gate_1",
191	        title="Gate 1 — Pre-Data Checkpoint",
192	        summary=(
193	            "Platform has completed Phase 0 verification. All structural prerequisites "
194	            "are in place before SCF corpus data enters the system. "
195	            "Approve to unlock data ingestion and begin Phase 1."
196	        ),
197	        checklist=checklist,
198	        run_id=state["run_id"],
199	    )
200	
201	    # interrupt() parks the graph and surfaces proposal to operator.
202	    # The operator's response dict is the return value of interrupt().
203	    return _run_gate(state, "gate_1", proposal)
204	
205	
206	# ── Gate 2 ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
207	
208	
209	def gate_2_node(state: HCGRCState) -> dict[str, Any]:
210	    """
211	    Gate 2 — Exploratory → Confirmatory transition.
212	
213	    The most consequential gate. The operator reviews the pre-registered
214	    hypothesis set and approves unlocking the test split. Uses ADR-0014
215	    Escalation infrastructure (GitHub issue → Claude → decision record).
216	
217	    Hard prerequisites (checked before interrupt fires):
218	      - DIVERGENCE-01 operationalization logged to Preregistration Ledger
219	      - Primary embedding model designated and logged
220	      - Hypothesis set complete and committed to PREREGISTRATION_LEDGER.md
221	      - Data split idempotency verified (data_split_verified == True)
222	    """
223	    # Verify hard prerequisites before surfacing this irreversible gate. Gate 2

content.text: Now let me check what the SAP says about stratification to see if that's required:

content.content:
### Stratification
Stratification variables are determined at data acquisition based on the actual distribution of the corpus. At minimum: SCF domain (33 classes) and STRM relationship type (5 classes). If domain × relationship type produces sparse cells, stratification falls back to domain only. The stratification decision is logged in the data steward's artifact before split execution.

### Seed Management
The random seed for all split operations is a single value set at first data acquisition and recorded in two places: the data steward's DVC artifact metadata and the pre-registration commit. **The seed is sacrosanct after pre-registration.** Changing it after the split is executed constitutes a SAP violation equivalent to running multiple splits and selecting the favorable one.

Seed selection method: generated from the RFC 3161 timestamp of the pre-registration commit (timestamp → SHA-256 → first 8 hex digits → integer). This makes the seed auditable and eliminates any possibility of seed selection bias.

content.text: This is important! The specification in 04_methods_scaffolding.md explicitly requires **stratification**, but the `compute_data_split()` function does not implement stratification at all. Let me check the docstring and comments more carefully:

1	"""
2	compute_data_split — deterministic, idempotent SCF corpus partitioning.
3	
4	Seed derivation: SHA-256 of the raw manifest file → int.from_bytes() → int seed.
5	Same manifest → identical seed → identical split on every independent run.
6	This is the idempotency guarantee tested in test_phase0/test_data_split.py.
7	
8	Split strategy: shuffle-and-slice on UNIQUE SCF control IDs (per SAP §7 / issue #60).
9	Semantically equivalent to GroupKFold when control_ids contains exactly one entry
10	per unique control. CALLER CONTRACT: control_ids must be deduplicated before calling —
11	one ID per control, not one row per STRM mapping. Passing mapping rows (280,000) instead
12	of control IDs (1,400) would produce leakage because set(train_ids) & set(test_ids) only
13	catches list-level duplicates, not the case where the same control's mapping rows land
14	in separate splits. The DataSplitAgent is responsible for deduplication before this call.
15	
16	No SCF control text appears in both train and test partitions (given the caller contract).
17	DataSplitAgent asserts len(set(train_ids) ∩ test_ids) == 0 before writing.
18	This assertion is logged and is a required Gate 1 artifact.
19	
20	Split ratios: 70% train / 15% validation / 15% test.
21	"""
22	
23	from __future__ import annotations
24	
25	import hashlib
26	from pathlib import Path
27	from typing import Any
28	
29	import numpy as np
30	
31	# ── Seed derivation ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
32	
33	
34	def derive_seed(manifest_path: Path | str) -> tuple[str, int]:
35	    """
36	    Derive a deterministic integer seed from the SHA-256 hash of a manifest file.
37	
38	    Returns:
39	        (manifest_hash_hex, seed_int)
40	    """
41	    path = Path(manifest_path)
42	    raw = path.read_bytes()
43	    h = hashlib.sha256(raw)
44	    manifest_hash = h.hexdigest()
45	    # 8 bytes (64-bit) → 1-in-2^64 collision probability between different manifests.
46	    # Using int.from_bytes on the raw digest (not the hex string) for portability.
47	    seed = int.from_bytes(h.digest()[:8], byteorder="big")
48	    return manifest_hash, seed

content.text: Good - the assertions are correctly implemented. Now let me check if there's any issue with the seeding approach. Let me look at the seed derivation more carefully:

content.content:
def derive_seed(manifest_path: Path | str) -> tuple[str, int]:
    """
    Derive a deterministic integer seed from the SHA-256 hash of a manifest file.

    Returns:
        (manifest_hash_hex, seed_int)
    """
    path = Path(manifest_path)
    raw = path.read_bytes()
    h = hashlib.sha256(raw)
    manifest_hash = h.hexdigest()
    # 8 bytes (64-bit) → 1-in-2^64 collision probability between different manifests.
    # Using int.from_bytes on the raw digest (not the hex string) for portability.
    seed = int.from_bytes(h.digest()[:8], byteorder="big")
    return manifest_hash, seed

### Stratification
Stratification variables are determined at data acquisition based on the actual distribution of the corpus. At minimum: SCF domain (33 classes) and STRM relationship type (5 classes). If domain × relationship type produces sparse cells, stratification falls back to domain only. The stratification decision is logged in the data steward's artifact before split execution.

### Seed Management
The random seed for all split operations is a single value set at first data acquisition and recorded in two places: the data steward's DVC artifact metadata and the pre-registration commit. **The seed is sacrosanct after pre-registration.** Changing it after the split is executed constitutes a SAP violation equivalent to running multiple splits and selecting the favorable one.

Seed selection method: generated from the RFC 3161 timestamp of the pre-registration commit (timestamp → SHA-256 → first 8 hex digits → integer). This makes the seed auditable and eliminates any possibility of seed selection bias.

### Test Set Access Control
The test split is physically inaccessible before Gate 2 (Orchestrator-enforced, LangGraph topology). The Data Steward agent writes the test split to an encrypted DVC artifact at split time. The decryption key is held by the Orchestrator and released only at Gate 2. All pre-Gate 2 evaluation runs — including model training, fine-tuning decisions, and exploratory analysis — use train + validation only.

Any analysis that touches the test split before Gate 2 invalidates the confirmatory analysis. This is not recoverable.

content.text:
There's no code enforcing that the seed becomes immutable after Gate 1. The seed is written to state, but nothing prevents a later agent from mutating `data_split_seed`. This could be a defect if any agent is inadvertently changing it. However, looking at the code, I don't see any agent that explicitly modifies `data_split_seed` after it's set. The LangGraph graph executes sequentially (based on what I see), so each agent should only write its own data.

Let me now look more carefully at what the critical spec issues might be. Let me re-read the docstring about seed derivation and the actual method used:

The spec says (line 4 of data_split.py): "Seed derivation: SHA-256 of the raw manifest file → int.from_bytes() → int seed."

But the SAP specification (04_methods_scaffolding.md) says: "Seed selection method: generated from the RFC 3161 timestamp of the pre-registration commit (timestamp → SHA-256 → first 8 hex digits → integer)."

These are DIFFERENT! The code uses SHA-256 of the manifest file, but the spec requires SHA-256 of the RFC 3161 timestamp of the pre-registration commit. Let me check if there's documentation explaining this discrepancy:

FOUND AN ISSUE! Look at ADR-0015 §75:

> "`compute_data_split()` derives its random seed exclusively from SHA-256 of the **data manifest hash**. No system entropy is used (no time-based seed, `os.urandom()`, or unseeded numpy random)."

But 04_methods_scaffolding.md says:

> "Seed selection method: generated from the RFC 3161 timestamp of the pre-registration commit (timestamp → SHA-256 → first 8 hex digits → integer)."

These are CONTRADICTORY! ADR-0015 says the seed comes from SHA-256 of the data manifest hash, but the SAP/Methods Scaffolding says it comes from RFC 3161 timestamp. The code implements the ADR-0015 version (manifest hash), not the Methods Scaffolding version (RFC 3161 timestamp).

Let me verify this is the real spec:

content.content:
1	# ADR-0015: Architecture Integrity Decisions
2	
3	**Status:** Accepted  
4	**Date:** 2026-06-10  
5	**Author:** Thomas Jones  
6	**Supersedes:** N/A  
7	**Relates to:** ADR-0011 (resilience), ADR-0012 (autonomous architecture), ADR-0014 (infrastructure)
8	
9	---
10	
11	## Context
12	
13	Adversarial review surfaced ten architecture integrity issues (#71–#80) spanning: structural firewall implementation, orchestrator failure model, state schema safety, data split integrity, checkpoint scale, agent evolution scope, inter-agent trust, observability correlation, and gate delivery mechanism. This ADR captures the resolution of all ten.
14	
15	---
16	
17	## Decisions
18	
19	### 1. Structural Firewall Claims Are Conditional on Phase 0 (#71)
20	
21	All claims that "the firewall between phases is structural, not procedural" — in ADR-0004, ADR-0007, README, and the Charter — are conditional on Phase 0 implementation completing before any data acquisition.
22	
23	**Phase 0 success criteria (hard prerequisites, added to GATES.md):**
24	- LangGraph skeleton running with all gate nodes present
25	- Gate 1 firing with synthetic payload confirmed
26	- PostgresSaver checkpointing confirmed on target hardware
27	- Gate 2 data split logic implemented and idempotency test passing
28	- No SCF data acquired until all criteria are met
29	
30	The structural firewall claim becomes true when Phase 0 completes. Not before.
31	
32	---
33	
34	### 2. Orchestrator Failure Model — Option B + Evolve Layer (#72)
35	
36	**Option B — Defensive gate nodes:** Each gate validates its own preconditions and rejects invalid state regardless of how T-00 routed. Gate nodes are the enforcement surface.
37	
38	**Evolve layer:** Gate rejection events are logged with full context (proposed action, rejection reason, state snapshot, run_id, timestamp) to a `failure_events` store. Agent Evolution monitors `failure_events` for patterns:
39	
40	- **Routing errors** (T-00 operational): Agent Evolution applies DSPy re-optimization to T-00's routing prompt autonomously. Logged to PROV-DM with before/after versions.
41	- **Architectural failures** (gate topology wrong, systematic design flaw): Escalation filed to operator.
42	
43	This makes the platform self-correcting for operational routing failures without requiring a permanent watchdog node. Option A (real-time watchdog) becomes redundant once T-00's routing stabilizes through the evolve loop.
44	
45	**Protected agent set:** Agent Evolution may not autonomously modify P1–P5, statistical-analyst, or hypothesis-formalizer prompts. See Decision 6.
46	
47	---
48	
49	### 3. Provenance Entry Ordering (#73)
50	
51	Every entry appended to `prov_trace`, `errors`, and `null_results` carries:
52	- `timestamp`: ISO 8601 UTC, generated at write time by the writing agent
53	- `source_id`: agent name + run_id + superstep number
54	
55	Downstream consumers sort by `timestamp` before processing. List position is never used as a proxy for execution order. Enforced by ARA Rigor Reviewer at validation time.
56	
57	---
58	
59	### 4. Gate Status Write Protection (#74)
60	
61	All `gate_status` writes go through `gate_coordinator_node`. No other node writes this key directly.
62	
63	**Phase 0 (linear topology) — implemented via merge reducer:**
64	`gate_status` is typed `Annotated[dict[GateID, GateStatus], lambda a, b: {**a, **b}]`. Each `gate_coordinator_node` call returns only the new `{gate_id: record}` entry; LangGraph merges it into the existing dict. This is safe under concurrent writes because dict-merge is commutative for non-overlapping keys (each gate writes a unique `gate_id`).
65	
66	`gate_coordinator_node` is a helper function called from within gate nodes — it is **not** a standalone LangGraph node in Phase 0. The ADR was written before implementation; the merge reducer approach was selected during adversarial review (F1/F4, 2026-06-11) as the correct Phase 0 mechanism.
67	
68	**Phase 1 (parallel topology) — to be implemented:**
69	When Phase 1 introduces parallel agent execution, the merge reducer alone cannot prevent two agents writing the same `gate_id` key simultaneously. Phase 1 will promote `gate_coordinator_node` to a true serialized LangGraph node with explicit edges from all gate-writing agents. Tracked in GitHub issue #103.
70	
71	**Topology enforcement (Decision 7 / #78) is a Phase 1 goal, not a Phase 0 guarantee.**
72	
73	---
74	
75	### 5. Data Split Idempotency — Deterministic Seed (#75)
76	
77	`compute_data_split()` derives its random seed exclusively from SHA-256 of the data manifest hash. No system entropy is used (no time-based seed, `os.urandom()`, or unseeded numpy random).
78	
79	**Required test (Phase 0 success criterion):** Run `compute_data_split()` twice with identical state. Assert bit-identical output. This test must pass before Gate 2 is considered operational.
80	
81	---
82	
83	### 6. Checkpoint Scale — Phase 0 Benchmark (#76)
84	
85	Hardware benchmarking is a Phase 0 deliverable, not a Phase 3 decision. Before Phase 3 implementation begins:
86	
87	1. Estimate checkpoint size per `HCGRCState` at analysis time
88	2. Benchmark PostgresSaver write throughput on Mac Mini compute node
89	3. Derive maximum feasible parallel branch count
90	4. Select `batch_size` accordingly — lock to SWARM_IMPLEMENTATION_ROADMAP.md
91	
92	`batch_size=50` is a placeholder until the benchmark runs. The result may require architectural adjustment.
93	
94	---
95	
96	### 7. Agent Evolution Scope — Protected Agent Set (#77)
97	
98	**Protected agents** (prompt modifications require Escalation approval before taking effect):
99	
100	| Agent | Reason |
101	|-------|--------|
102	| p1-strm-nlp | Defines NLP similarity measurement approach |
103	| p2-domain-clustering | Defines clustering methodology |
104	| p3-coverage-diffusion | Defines semantic coverage measurement |
105	| p4-gap-analysis | Defines gap analysis methodology |
106	| p5-risk-quantification | Defines risk quantification approach |
107	| statistical-analyst | Defines how evidence is weighted and tested |
108	| hypothesis-formalizer | Defines how hypotheses are structured for testing |
109	
110	Modifying these agents' prompts mid-tier against SCF corpus data is analytically equivalent to changing the research design post-hoc — a form of data snooping that bypasses Gate 2.
111	
112	**Legitimate evolution paths for protected agents:**
113	
114	1. **Before Gate 1:** DSPy optimization against synthetic data or public benchmarks only. No SCF data in system yet.
115	2. **Mid-tier via Escalation:** Platform files structured proposal with specific change and rationale. Operator reviews via Claude. Decision logged before change takes effect.
116	3. **Between tiers:** Post-tier retrospective informs deliberate redesign. Changes committed and version-bumped before next tier starts. This is science working correctly — Tier 1 findings inform Tier 2 instrument design.
117	
118	**Agent Evolution scope:** Fully autonomous for all non-protected agents (T-00 routing, ingestion, reporting, QA, infrastructure).
119	
120	---
121	
122	### 8. Inter-Agent Trust — Topology Enforcement (#78)
123	
124	**Phase 1 — Option A (structural):** LangGraph edge definitions control which agents connect to which state keys. An agent not wired to write a particular key cannot reach it — enforced at graph compile time, zero runtime overhead.
125	
126	**Phase 2 — Option B (runtime assertions):** Deferred. Tracked in sub-issue #96. Activate when: a state corruption event occurs that topology enforcement did not catch, or Tier 2 scale requires finer-grained write control.
127	
128	---
129	
130	### 9. Observability Correlation — Common run_id (#79)
131	
132	A single `run_id` is generated at the start of each research run and propagated to all four observability stores:
133	
134	| Store | Integration |
135	|-------|------------|
136	| MLflow | `run_id` as experiment tag |
137	| Phoenix | `run_id` as trace attribute |
138	| W3C PROV-DM | `run_id` as activity identifier |
139	| PostgresSaver | `run_id` as thread_id / checkpoint key |
140	
141	Cross-store correlation is a single-key lookup. A `RUNBOOK.md` documents the explicit cross-store query sequence for common diagnostic scenarios. No custom query infrastructure built.
142	
143	---
144	
145	### 10. Gate 2 Delivery — Escalation Loop (#80)
146	
147	Gate 2 interrupt uses the Escalation loop established in ADR-0014. No separate UI required.
148	
149	The Gate 2 payload (split parameters, summary statistics) travels via GitHub issue — not raw SCF data. Data stays on the compute node. The operator reviews summary artifacts, not the dataset. Local-first constraint (ADR-0002) maintained.
150	
151	---
152	
153	## Consequences
154	
155	**What this enables:**
156	- Phase 0 can proceed with a complete, unambiguous success criteria checklist
157	- Orchestrator failures are self-correcting without permanent watchdog overhead
158	- Gate integrity is structurally enforced, not convention-dependent
159	- Research instrument (P1–P5) integrity is protected against silent prompt drift
160	- Cross-store diagnostics are operationally feasible for a single-operator program
161	- Gate 2 approval requires no new infrastructure
162	
163	**What this closes:**
164	- #71 through #80: all ten adversarial architecture findings resolved
165	
166	**What this creates:**
167	- #96: runtime assertion guards (Phase 2, sub-issue of #78)
168	- Phase 0 success criteria expanded with: idempotency test, hardware benchmark, Gate 1 structural test
169	
170	**Documents requiring update:**
171	- GATES.md: Phase 0 prerequisites, protected agent set, gate coordinator node
172	- SWARM_IMPLEMENTATION_ROADMAP.md: batch_size benchmark as Phase 0 deliverable
173	- Agent Evolution AGENT.md: protected agent set constraint
174	- P1–P5 and statistical-analyst, hypothesis-formalizer AGENT.md cards: behavioral constraint on prompt modification
175	- RUNBOOK.md: create with common run_id cross-store query procedures
176

The Methods Scaffolding says "Seed selection method: generated from the RFC 3161 timestamp" but ADR-0015 was accepted on 2026-06-10 (per line 4 of ADR-0015), which is more recent than anything mentioned in the methods scaffolding. So there's a SPEC MISMATCH.

This is a genuine defect: **The spec in 04_methods_scaffolding.md says the seed should come from RFC 3161 timestamp, but ADR-0015 (the accepted decision) says it comes from the data manifest hash, and the code implements ADR-0015.**

Now let me check if this is critical or just a documentation issue. Let me see what the actual impact is:

The SAP specification says seed selection must be from "RFC 3161 timestamp of the pre-registration commit." But:
- ADR-0015 supersedes this with "data manifest hash"
- The code implements ADR-0015 (data manifest hash)
- The Phase 0 code uses a hardcoded seed or state value (neither RFC 3161 nor data manifest)

This is a SPEC MISALIGNMENT that needs to be reported. Let me now check if there are any other issues with the implementation by carefully reviewing the critical sections again.

The split logic works correctly - all elements are accounted for. The ratios match the specification for the actual corpus size. Now let me summarize my findings.

Based on my deep audit of the data-split-node unit (src/nodes/data_split.py), here are my findings:

## SPEC MISALIGNMENT FINDINGS:

1. **Seed Derivation Method Contradiction**: The code implements seed derivation from SHA-256 of the data manifest (per ADR-0015 §75), but docs/protocol/04_methods_scaffolding.md specifies seed derivation from RFC 3161 timestamp of the pre-registration commit. These are fundamentally different approaches. Additionally, the Phase 0 implementation uses a hardcoded seed (42) or state value, which matches neither specification.

The actual specifications conflict:
- Code + ADR-0015: "SHA-256 of the data manifest hash"
- Methods Scaffolding: "RFC 3161 timestamp of pre-registration commit (timestamp → SHA-256 → first 8 hex digits → integer)"

2. **Documentation Misstatement**: Line 17 of data_split.py states "DataSplitAgent asserts len(set(train_ids) ∩ test_ids) == 0 before writing" but in Phase 0, data_split_node itself makes this assertion (lines 113-121), not DataStewardAgent. DataStewardAgent is Phase 1+. This is misleading documentation.

3. **Missing Stratification in Phase 0**: Methods Scaffolding §1 requires "Stratification variables... At minimum: SCF domain (33 classes) and STRM relationship type (5 classes)," but compute_data_split() implements simple shuffle-and-slice with no stratification. However, this appears intentional for Phase 0 (synthetic data) with stratification deferred to DataStewardAgent in Phase 1.

## CORRECTNESS FINDINGS:

All actual logic is correct:
- Leakage assertions correctly verify no ID appears in multiple splits ✓
- Split sizes sum to total control count ✓
- Split ratios match specification within test tolerances (70%±2%, 15%±2%) ✓
- SHA-256 seed derivation is deterministic and idempotent ✓
- Caller contract enforcement (control IDs vs mapping rows) properly documented ✓

findings.title: Seed Derivation Method Specification Conflict
findings.problem: The code implements seed derivation from SHA-256 of the data manifest (per ADR-0015 §75, line 77), but docs/protocol/04_methods_scaffolding.md (Seed Management section) specifies the seed must be 'generated from the RFC 3161 timestamp of the pre-registration commit (timestamp → SHA-256 → first 8 hex digits → integer)'. These are fundamentally different approaches. The two specifications cannot both be correct. Additionally, Phase 0 uses a hardcoded seed (42) or state value, matching neither specification.
findings.spec_or_invariant: 04_methods_scaffolding.md §Seed Management: 'Seed selection method: generated from the RFC 3161 timestamp of the pre-registration commit'. Contradicted by ADR-0015 §75: 'compute_data_split() derives its random seed exclusively from SHA-256 of the data manifest hash.'
findings.proposed_fix: Reconcile the conflicting seed derivation specifications. Either: (1) update Methods Scaffolding to align with the ADR-0015 decision (manifest hash), explicitly noting that this supersedes the prior timestamp specification, (2) implement RFC 3161 timestamp-based seeding as specified in Methods Scaffolding and update ADR-0015, or (3) document the rationale for why Phase 0 uses hardcoded seeds while Phase 1+ follow a different approach. The seed method is a critical research integrity property that must be unambiguous.

findings.title: Inaccurate Documentation of Split Assertion Location
findings.problem: The module docstring states 'DataSplitAgent asserts len(set(train_ids) ∩ test_ids) == 0 before writing.' However, in Phase 0 (the current implementation), data_split_node itself makes the assertion (lines 113-121), not DataStewardAgent. DataStewardAgent is a Phase 1+ agent that has not been implemented yet. The docstring is Phase 1-focused but misleadingly describes the Phase 0 assertion location.
findings.spec_or_invariant: src/nodes/data_split.py docstring and src/nodes/data_split.py:17 claim DataStewardAgent performs the assertion, but src/nodes/data_split.py:113-121 shows data_split_node performs it in Phase 0.
findings.proposed_fix: Clarify the docstring to state: 'In Phase 0, data_split_node asserts len(set(train_ids) ∩ test_ids) == 0 (lines 113-121). In Phase 1+, DataStewardAgent performs equivalent assertion before writing.'

findings.title: Missing Stratification Implementation for Non-Synthetic Splits
findings.problem: Methods Scaffolding §Data Split Strategy specifies: 'Stratification variables are determined at data acquisition based on the actual distribution of the corpus. At minimum: SCF domain (33 classes) and STRM relationship type (5 classes).' However, compute_data_split() implements only simple shuffle-and-slice with no stratification capability. The function has no stratify_by parameter and does not support stratified random split. This is appropriate for Phase 0 (synthetic data), but the function will need stratification capability when called from Phase 1 with real data (when manifest_path is provided).
findings.spec_or_invariant: docs/protocol/04_methods_scaffolding.md §Data Split Strategy §Stratification: 'Stratification variables are determined at data acquisition based on the actual distribution of the corpus. At minimum: SCF domain (33 classes) and STRM relationship type (5 classes). If domain × relationship type produces sparse cells, stratification falls back to domain only.'
findings.proposed_fix: Either: (1) add optional stratify_by parameter to compute_data_split() to support stratified splitting on specified columns (deferred to Phase 1 implementation when manifest_path is used), or (2) explicitly document in the function docstring that stratification is deferred to DataStewardAgent in Phase 1, and compute_data_split() is Phase 0 only. The SAP requirement is clear; either the function needs the capability or the implementation must be split between Phase 0 and Phase 1 calls.
